title: A protocol to determine the least cost supply of coal to China with an installation-level optimization model
author: Gosens, Turnbull
description: SummaryThis protocol provides instructions on how to run a linear optimization model that determines the cost-optimal supply of coal, from Chinese and foreign mines, to satisfy a given demand for coal in Chinese power and steel plants. We describe how to download the code and required software and detail steps to insert third-party data. We further provide instructions to adjust and define the scenario settings, build and solve the optimization problem file, and visualize the model outcomes.For complete details on the use and execution of this protocol, please refer to Gosens et al. (2022).1

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: This protocol provides instructions on how to run the “Installation-Level China Coal Model (IL-CCM)”,2 a linear optimization model that determines the cost-optimal supply of thermal and coking coal to Chinese power plants and steel plants. This model was used in a study that assessed how China’s decarbonization and energy security plans are set to reduce its future seaborne coal imports.1

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: Understanding the Chinese market is of key relevance to understanding international trade in coal. China is the largest importer of both thermal coal (used for power generation) and coking coal (used for steelmaking), being responsible for roughly a quarter of total global imports of both types of coal.3,4 Further, China itself produces vast quantities of coal itself. Over 2019–2021, a little over 50 percent of global coal production came from Chinese mines.4 China’s future coal imports are subject to two pressures. First, China has announced a net-zero emission target for 2060 with an interim target of peaking carbon emissions by 2030, and this will inevitably involve reduced consumption of coal.5,6 Second, China has been seeking to improve its energy security, or the share of energy derived from domestic sources, by opening up additional coal mining capacity, and improving the transport infrastructure to get this coal to its domestic power and steel plants.7

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: Determining the likely volumes of coal shipped from any mine, or from any country, to the Chinese market, requires understanding the relative competitiveness between these suppliers. A common approach to modelling such relative competitiveness is with so-called “multiregional coal supply models”, with previous applications to global coal markets,8,9,10,11 and a long list of China-focused coal market analyses.7,12,13,14,15,16,17,18

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: The regions, or nodes, in such models can either produce or consume coal (or both), and are connected with other nodes through links, which represent transport infrastructure, and allow one node to supply coal demand in another node. These models include information on coal production capacity and cost for each producing node (in coal mines), transport capacity and cost for each link (railways, roads, waterways, and trans-shipment points), and coal demand levels in consuming nodes.

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: In comparison to these previous models, the IL-CCM stands out because of its very high geo-spatial resolution. The most detailed previous analysis with this modelling method represented China’s coal production and demand conflated into provincial-level nodes, and the transport system conflated into inter-provincial links, for a total of 31 nodes and about 150 links.7 The IL-CCM has installation-level location and technical detail for each individual coal mine, power and steel plant, as well as for all connecting rail, highway, UHV transmission, and shipping networks, for a total of 12,161 nodes and 40,396 links (for more detail see “Methods S1”). A backcasting exercise shows that this high level of detail greatly helps model accuracy. The model predicts levels of Chinese coal imports from different suppliers that very closely match historically observed levels (Figure 1).Figure 1IL-CCM model prediction accuracy in backcasting exercise: Historical (real) and predicted (model) levels of Chinese thermal and coking coal imports, by origin, for the years 2015–2019

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: The IL-CCM model uses input data stored in spreadsheet files. Data preparation, processing of the solution file created by the optimization software, and visualization of results is done in R. The file with the linear problem written out as a set of mathematical expressions (a .lp file), for use in linear optimization, is created in Jupyter on a cloud computing instance, whilst solving the problem file is done locally with the CPLEX solver.

steps_raw.title: Before you begin
steps_raw.instruction: We provide all code and data used in this protocol in a public repository, whereas earlier work with similar methods, at best, provided only mathematical descriptions of the optimization model,7,8 or in one exception, model descriptions and data,10 and is the first Star Protocol reporting a process for an energy system optimization model. This repository further includes scripts for editing the networks, and local and freeware alternatives for the optimization steps. The user will need a dataset on mine-level production costs and capacity. This is commercial data that could not be provided with the public repository. For further model details including a full mathematical description, see “Methods S1”.
